title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra — Water Footprint of Crops (Report 47)
notes: General: Comprehensive peer-reviewed global crop water footprint database; primary source for most water_per_kg values.
Almonds — water_per_kg: 13,080 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect California study (source 7, 10,240 L/kg) → final 11,660 L/kg.

Lentils — water_per_kg: 1,250 L/kg (global weighted average; Report 47 Table A1). Previous dataset value of 5,874 L/kg was a clear error (4.7× too high), corrected 2026-05-21. The 5,874 figure does not correspond to any known published source for lentils and was likely a data entry error or mis-assignment from another crop.

Rice — water_per_kg: Report 47 Table A1 gives 1,673 L/kg for paddy rice. Dataset uses 2,500 L/kg which reflects milled white rice basis: 1,673 ÷ 0.65 milling yield ≈ 2,574 L/kg. This is consistent with Chapagain & Hoekstra (2011) who report 2,291–2,500 L/kg for white rice. The 2,500 value is accepted as correct for the milled-rice basis used throughout the dataset.

Lettuce — water_per_kg: 130 L/kg used (sourced from WFN online crop database). Report 47 Table A1 reports 237 L/kg. The discrepancy reflects different methodologies: Report 47 uses a global production-weighted virtual water model; the WFN online calculator (130 L/kg) applies simplified blue+green accounting. Neither is clearly wrong; the lower figure may reflect the dominant role of intensive irrigation-efficient greenhouse production (particularly in China). Value flagged but not corrected — difference is within methodological range.

Cucumber — water_per_kg: 240 L/kg used. Report 47 Table A1 reports 353 L/kg. Dataset figure is 32% lower than the peer-reviewed value. The discrepancy may reflect greenhouse production dominance (China, Netherlands) which achieves higher yields per m³. Value is in a plausible range; flagged but not corrected.
Walnuts — water_per_kg: 7,744 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 9,279 L/kg) → final 8,512 L/kg.
Pistachios — water_per_kg: 10,697 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 11,356 L/kg) → final 11,027 L/kg.
Hazelnuts — water_per_kg: 9,807 L/kg; averaged with ScienceDirect tree nuts study (source 34, 10,511 L/kg) → final 10,159 L/kg.

Peanuts — water_per_kg: 3,740 L/kg; averaged with secondary sources → final 3,537 L/kg. Report 47 gives 2,782 L/kg for in-shell peanuts; 3,740 reflects shelled/edible kernel basis after accounting for ~74% kernel recovery. Difference is within expected range for basis discrepancy.
Sweet potato — water_per_kg: 461 L/kg (global weighted average, primarily rain-fed tropical production).
Chickpeas — water_per_kg: 4,325 L/kg (global weighted average, includes irrigation and green water; high due to arid growing regions).

Barley — water_per_kg: 1,423 L/kg (global average; temperate grain, primarily rain-fed).

Orange — water_per_kg: 460 L/kg (global average; largely irrigated Mediterranean and subtropical production).
Garlic — water_per_kg: 656 L/kg (global average; irrigated production in China, India, US).

title: The Poultry Site — Layer Hen Feed and Production Over Lifetime

Layer hen — lifetime egg output: ~300 eggs ≈ 17.1 kg eggs per bird over productive life.
Layer hen — lifetime FCR: 69 ÷ 17.1 ≈ 4.04 kg feed per kg eggs.

title: Dairy Global — Feed Efficiency in Dairy Cows
notes: Dairy cow — lifetime feed breakdown: ~36,450 kg lactation feed (1.35 kg/kg milk × 27,000 kg milk) + ~2,700 kg dry-period feed (3 periods × 60 days × 15 kg/day) + ~5,840 kg heifer rearing (730 days × 8 kg DM/day) = ~44,990 kg total.
Dairy cow — lifetime milk output: ~27,000 kg per cow over productive life.
Dairy cow — lifetime FCR: 44,990 ÷ 27,000 ≈ 1.67 kg feed per kg milk.

title: EA Forum — Neuron Count Measures May Underweight Suffering in Farmed Fish
notes: Atlantic salmon — neuron_count: value used is 130,000,000 (130 million), derived as follows.

The commonly cited figure of 10 million neurons for salmon originates from an empirical count of a 0.75g zebrafish. Applying this to a ~3,000g Atlantic salmon is biologically indefensible — the figure does not scale with body or brain size.

Bruns & Davies (2022) ran a Monte Carlo simulation estimating 8–64 million neurons for salmon-sized fish, but this range was derived from data on fish under 4g and extrapolated across a >750× weight gap, so the uncertainty is substantial.

A scaling-adjusted estimate: applying Invincible Wellbeing's vertebrate curve-fitting formula to a 3,000g salmon yields ~1.9 billion neurons, but fish brains are roughly 1/15th the relative size of mammals and birds at equivalent body weight. Dividing by 15 gives ~130 million, which is the value used here.

All estimates carry significant uncertainty. The 130M figure is the most biologically grounded available estimate given the absence of direct neurological counts for Atlantic salmon at commercial harvest size.

title: Wikipedia — Domestic Turkey
notes: Turkey — weight_kg: Commercial tom turkeys at slaughter typically weigh 11–13 kg live weight; hens 6–8 kg. Value of 10 kg used as a weighted average reflecting the commercial flock mix.

Turkey — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for turkeys is available in the literature. As a closely related galliform to the domestic chicken, an estimate of ~262 million neurons is applied, slightly above the 221 million reported for chickens (Herculano-Houzel methodology), consistent with turkey brain mass being moderately larger than chicken brain mass at commercial slaughter size.

title: The Poultry Site — Turkey Production and Feed Conversion
notes: Turkey — FCR: Commercial turkeys achieve ~2.5–2.7 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 2.6 used.
Turkey — dressing percentage: ~80% for commercial toms, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.
Turkey — feed composition: Similar to broiler chicken — corn 55–60%, soybean meal 20–25%, wheat 8–12% of diet by weight.
Turkey — feed split applied: corn 60% × 2.6 FCR / 0.80 dressing = 1.95 kg corn/kg meat; soy 22% = 0.71 kg; wheat 10% = 0.33 kg per kg turkey meat.

title: PMC — Nervous Systems of Decapod Crustaceans
notes: Shrimp — neuron_count: Decapod crustaceans (including penaeoid shrimp) have been estimated at approximately 100,000–1,000,000 neurons in the central nervous system, with most commercial penaeid shrimp species falling near the lower end (~100,000–500,000). A value of 1,000,000 is used here as a conservative upper bound for commercial-sized shrimp (~15–20g), consistent with estimates for small decapods in the peer-reviewed literature.

title: WWF — Bycatch Overview
notes: Shrimp trawling — bycatch_amount: Gulf of Mexico and Southeast US shrimp trawl fisheries have historically reported bycatch-to-shrimp ratios of 3–10 kg discarded finfish per kg shrimp landed, with a commonly cited average of ~5.7 kg bycatch per kg shrimp.

Shrimp trawling — bycatch composition: Bycatch consists predominantly of small demersal and pelagic finfish, sea turtles (incidental), and invertebrates. Finfish bycatch is represented here using sardines (animal_id=10) as a proxy for small pelagic and demersal finfish.

Shrimp — wild vs. farmed: Approximately 55% of global shrimp production is wild-caught (predominantly trawl). The bycatch values apply to the wild-caught fraction. Farmed shrimp contribute no trawl bycatch but have separate feed and environmental impacts.

title: Wikipedia — Sardine
notes: Sardines — weight_kg: Commercial sardines (Sardina pilchardus, Sardinops sagax) at harvest typically weigh 50–150g; a value of 0.1 kg (100g) is used as representative.

Sardines — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for sardines exists in the peer-reviewed literature. As small teleost fish with brain structure comparable to other clupeoids, an estimate of ~10 million neurons is applied, consistent with figures used for small pelagic fish of similar body size (e.g., reference values for small fish in the range of 5–30 million neurons). This carries significant uncertainty.

title: FAO — The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture 2018
notes: Sardines — bycatch: Sardines are harvested primarily by purse seine, which is a highly selective method with bycatch rates generally below 5–10% of total catch weight, substantially lower than demersal trawl fisheries.

Sardines — bycatch_amount: A value of 0.1 kg bycatch per kg sardines landed is used, reflecting the low but non-zero incidental catch typical of small pelagic purse seine operations (primarily other small schooling fish and occasional juvenile finfish).

Sardines — bycatch_animal_id: Bycatch represented using tuna (animal_id=5) as a proxy for larger incidental finfish catch in pelagic fisheries, though actual bycatch species composition varies by region and season.

title: Wikipedia — Sheep
notes: Lamb — weight_kg: Commercial lambs are typically slaughtered at 40–55 kg live weight; a value of 45 kg is used.

Lamb — neuron_count: Sheep have been estimated at approximately 1.2 billion neurons based on isotropic fractionator data for ovine brain (Herculano-Houzel methodology). Sheep brain mass (~140g) is substantially smaller than cattle (~500g), consistent with the proportionally lower neuron count relative to beef cattle (~3 billion).

title: MLA — Sheep Feed and Nutrition
notes: Lamb — FCR: Commercial lambs on feedlot finishing achieve ~4.5–5.5 kg feed per kg live weight gain; value of 5.0 used for feedlot lamb.
Lamb — dressing percentage: ~50% for commercial lamb, used to convert FCR to kg feed per kg dressed meat.
Lamb — feed composition: Finishing rations typically include corn 40–50%, soy 10–15%, grass hay 20–30%, alfalfa 10–20% by weight.
Lamb — feed split applied: corn 45% × 5.0 FCR / 0.50 dressing = 4.5 kg corn/kg meat; soy 12% = 1.2 kg; hay 28% = 2.8 kg; alfalfa 15% = 1.5 kg per kg lamb meat.

title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
notes: Farmed shrimp — feed composition: Commercial shrimp feeds typically contain soybean meal 25–35% and wheat 15–25% as primary plant-based ingredients; fishmeal provides 10–25% (animal origin, not tracked here).
Farmed shrimp — FCR: Modern semi-intensive and intensive penaeid shrimp aquaculture achieves FCR of ~1.3–1.6 kg feed per kg live shrimp; value of 1.5 used.
Farmed shrimp — dressing percentage: ~67% (heads-on whole shrimp converted to headless); plant feed values computed per kg headless shrimp.
Farmed shrimp — feed split applied: soy 30% × 1.5 / 0.67 = 0.67 kg soy/kg shrimp; wheat 20% × 1.5 / 0.67 = 0.45 kg wheat/kg shrimp. Applies to farmed fraction (~45% of global supply) only.

title: Dairy Global — Milk-to-Yogurt Conversion Ratio
notes: Yogurt milk-to-product conversion: Standard plain yogurt production requires approximately 1.1–1.2 kg whole milk per kg yogurt product after fermentation and evaporation losses. A factor of 1.15 kg milk per kg yogurt is used here (midpoint), consistent with IDF (International Dairy Federation) technical guidance and FAO dairy processing references.

Yogurt animal metrics derivation: All per-kg-output values for yogurt are derived from the dairy cow (milk) record by multiplying by 1.15 (the milk-per-yogurt conversion factor). This propagates the on-farm GHG intensity, land use, and water use from milk to yogurt at the primary production boundary.
pasture_ha_per_kg_yogurt = milk_value × 1.15 = 0.0001 × 1.15 = 0.000115 ha/kg.
ch4 (P&N): 0.24 × 1.15 = 0.276 kg CH4/kg yogurt.
ch4 (GLEAM): 0.22 × 1.15 = 0.253 kg CH4/kg yogurt.
n2o (GLEAM): 0.004 × 1.15 = 0.0046 kg N2O/kg yogurt.
co2 (P&N): 0.08 × 1.15 = 0.092 kg CO2/kg yogurt.
co2 (GLEAM): 0.07 × 1.15 = 0.0805 kg CO2/kg yogurt.
yield_fraction: 1.0 (conversion already expressed per kg yogurt as consumed).
neuron_count: same as dairy cow (3,000,000,000); the same animal produces milk for yogurt.
weight_kg: same as dairy cow (680 kg).

title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra (2012) — Water Footprint of Animal Products (Report 48, Vol. 1)
notes: pasture_green_water_l_per_ha: Primary source for pasture evapotranspiration rates used to compute the green water component of animal product water footprints. Report 48 uses CRU TS 2.1 gridded climate data and the WaterGAP2 hydrological model to estimate green water ET on permanent pasture at 5×5 arcminute resolution globally.

Beef/dairy pasture ET: global production-weighted average of approximately 6,500,000 L/ha/yr (650 mm/yr), reflecting a mix of tropical high-ET pastures (Brazil, sub-Saharan Africa: 800–1,200 mm/yr) and temperate lower-ET systems (US, EU, Argentina: 400–700 mm/yr). Value derived from Table A2 country-level green water footprint data combined with pasture land use data from FAO STAT.

Lamb/sheep pasture ET: global production-weighted average of approximately 5,500,000 L/ha/yr (550 mm/yr), reflecting the dominant role of temperate grassland systems in New Zealand, UK, and Australia (all 400–700 mm/yr) in global sheep meat and wool production. Confidence 2 — significant regional variation; temperate systems dominate sheep production unlike beef.

Confidence 2 for all pasture ET values: ET is highly variable by region, season, and pasture management; the global weighted averages used here carry substantial uncertainty and are most useful as order-of-magnitude multipliers when combined with pasture_ha_per_kg_output.

title: Allen et al. (1998) — FAO Irrigation and Drainage Paper No. 56: Crop Evapotranspiration
notes: pasture_green_water_l_per_ha: Secondary source confirming global reference evapotranspiration (ET0) ranges for grassland and permanent pasture. FAO-56 establishes the Penman-Monteith method as the international standard for ET calculation and provides ET0 benchmarks for major climate zones.

Temperate grassland ET0 (Table 4 and regional examples): 400–700 mm/yr, consistent with the low end of the Report 48 pasture ET range used for sheep-dominant systems (New Zealand, UK, Northern Europe, Patagonian Argentina).
Tropical pasture ET0: 700–1,200 mm/yr (sub-Saharan Africa, tropical Brazil, Southeast Asia), consistent with the high end driving the global beef average upward.

Global grassland average: The FAO-56 methodology yields a global area-weighted ET of approximately 600–700 mm/yr for permanent pasture, corroborating the 650 mm/yr (6,500,000 L/ha/yr) value used for beef and dairy and the 550 mm/yr (5,500,000 L/ha/yr) value used for lamb.
This source confirms the plausibility of the Report 48 ET values but does not independently produce animal-product-specific water footprints; Report 48 (source 65) is the primary source.

title: Mekonnen & Hoekstra (2010) — The Green, Blue and Grey Water Footprint of Crops and Crop Products (UNESCO-IHE Report 47)
notes: Primary source for green_water_per_kg and blue_water_per_kg splits for plant foods. Reports global average green, blue, and grey water footprints per crop in m³/ton (= L/kg) based on a grid-based model covering 1996–2005 production data at 5×5 arcminute resolution.
Green water = evapotranspiration from soil moisture recharged by rainfall (no human extraction). Blue water = evapotranspiration of irrigation water drawn from surface/groundwater.

Values used here are global production-weighted averages from Table A1. Confidence 3 for major staple crops (wheat, corn, rice, soy, sorghum, potato) where M&H have dense country coverage. Confidence 2 for tree nuts and specialty crops where regional variation is high or M&H coverage is thinner.

title: FAO (2013) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Pig and Chicken Supply Chains
notes: Global pig feed composition: FAO GLEAM pig report documents production-weighted average finishing-diet composition across major producing regions (China, EU, US, Brazil). Global average: corn ~55–60%, wheat ~10–15%, soybean meal ~20–25%, with minor barley, cassava, and by-products.

Pork — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: Global swine FCR ~2.8 kg/kg live weight (consistent with source 31); dressing ~73%; feed/kg pork = 3.836 kg. Wheat fraction ~12% of global average diet (higher in EU ~35–45%, lower in US/Brazil <5%); wheat feed = 3.836 × 0.12 = 0.46 kg/kg pork. Confidence 3 — wheat inclusion varies substantially by region; global average reflects production-weighted blend of corn-dominant (Americas) and wheat-dominant (EU) systems.

Global broiler feed composition: Corn ~55–60%, soybean meal ~20–25%, wheat ~8–12% (wheat use highest in EU and Middle East broiler production), minor sorghum, fats, vitamins. US production is corn-dominant with wheat <5%; EU production uses wheat 20–35%.

Chicken — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: Global broiler FCR ~1.8 kg/kg live weight; dressing ~75% (source 54); feed/kg chicken = 2.40 kg. Wheat fraction ~10% of global average diet; wheat feed = 2.40 × 0.10 = 0.24 kg/kg chicken. Confidence 3 — wheat inclusion varies substantially by region; global average reflects production-weighted blend of corn-dominant (Americas/Asia) and wheat-significant (EU) systems.

title: Leinonen et al. (2012) — Predicting the Environmental Impacts of Chicken Production in the EU (Poultry Science)
notes: Broiler chicken — feed composition (EU): Typical EU broiler finishing diet contains wheat 20–30%, corn 20–35%, soybean meal 20–25%, with remainder from sunflower, rapeseed, and minor ingredients. Wheat inclusion rates higher than US due to greater availability and lower cost relative to corn in European markets.
Broiler chicken — FCR: EU commercial broilers achieve FCR 1.7–2.0 kg/kg live weight; consistent with global average FCR ~1.8 used in this dataset. Dressing percentage ~75% (source 54).

Broiler chicken — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: EU wheat fraction ~25%; applied to global production-weighted average of ~10% wheat (EU production ~20% of global volume); this supports the 0.24 kg wheat/kg chicken entry derived from source 68.

Pig — FCR verification: EU commercial pig finishing achieves FCR ~2.6–3.0 kg/kg live weight, consistent with global FCR 2.8 (source 31). EU diet typically 40–50% wheat, 15–20% barley, 20–25% soy, confirming wheat as a significant global pork feed component.

title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
notes: Greenhouse gas emissions (kg CO2e per kg food) published by Our World in Data, sourcing Poore & Nemecek (2018) Science doi:10.1126/science.aaq0216. Values represent global weighted averages from farm to farm gate, including land use change, fertilizer N2O, farm energy, and enteric/manure emissions where applicable. Key vegetable category medians from OWID dataset: Tomatoes 2.09 kg CO2e/kg; Potatoes 0.46 kg CO2e/kg; Onions & Leeks 0.50 kg CO2e/kg; Brassicas 0.51 kg CO2e/kg; Root Vegetables 0.43 kg CO2e/kg; Other Vegetables 0.53 kg CO2e/kg. Legume categories from OWID P&N 2018 dataset: Peas (incl. lentils and chickpeas) 0.98 kg CO2e/kg; Other Pulses (incl. black beans and other dry Phaseolus vulgaris beans) 1.79 kg CO2e/kg.

Soybeans — emissions_per_kg: ~3.0 kg CO2e/kg (farm gate, global weighted average including LUC; P&N 2018 does not list raw soybeans as a directly consumed food — OWID dataset covers soy products: Tofu 3.16 kg CO2e/kg and Soy milk 0.98 kg CO2e/kg; raw soybeans as a commodity crop estimated at ~3.0 kg CO2e/kg based on P&N global farm-gate range including deforestation-driven LUC; the higher 3.0 figure vs earlier 2.0 estimate reflects correct production-weighting of Brazilian origin (high LUC intensity) in global soy supply).

Lentils — emissions_per_kg: OWID 'Peas' category mean is 0.98 kg CO2e/kg (encompasses lentils, peas, and other small legumes globally). The lentil-specific P&N 2018 supplementary estimate is ~1.6 kg CO2e/kg, higher than the broad category mean because lentil production in semi-arid regions (Canada, India, Turkey) generates higher per-unit N2O from residue decomposition. The crop-specific figure of 1.6 is used in legumes.json in preference to the broader category mean.

Chickpeas — emissions_per_kg: OWID 'Peas' category mean is 0.98 kg CO2e/kg (chickpeas grouped with other small legumes). Chickpea-specific P&N 2018 estimate is ~0.86 kg CO2e/kg, slightly below the 'Peas' category average due to chickpeas' dryland rain-fed production profile with minimal irrigation energy and synthetic N use.

Other Pulses incl. black beans — emissions_per_kg: OWID 'Other Pulses' category is 1.79 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018; covers Phaseolus vulgaris dry beans incl. black beans, kidney beans, navy beans; higher than 'Peas' category due to higher cultivation intensity and LUC exposure in Brazil and Central America). Black-bean-specific P&N supplementary estimate is ~2.0 kg CO2e/kg.
Wheat — emissions_per_kg: 1.57 kg CO2e/kg (global weighted average; includes fertilizer N2O, farm energy, land use change).
Corn (Maize) — emissions_per_kg: 1.7 kg CO2e/kg (global weighted average; includes fertilizer N2O, farm energy, land use change).

Rice (paddy) — emissions_per_kg: 2.7 kg CO2e/kg (global weighted average; notably high due to CH4 from anaerobic decomposition in flooded paddy fields, which contributes ~50% of total rice GHG footprint).
Oats — emissions_per_kg: 1.58 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 groups oats with wheat/rye as temperate small grains; value reflects the same fertilizer N2O and land use profile).
Barley — emissions_per_kg: 1.5 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 global median for barley as a temperate cereal; slightly lower than wheat due to lower average nitrogen application rates).
Sorghum — emissions_per_kg: 1.4 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 range for dryland cereals; sorghum uses less nitrogen than corn and is largely rain-fed, placing it at the lower end of the cereal GHG range).

Almonds — emissions_per_kg: 2.3 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts global weighted average; covers farm-gate GHG including irrigation energy, fertilizer N2O, and orchard management; land use change contribution is low for established California/Mediterranean orchards).
Walnuts — emissions_per_kg: 2.3 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts global weighted average; temperate deciduous orchard; farm-gate boundary including fertilizer N2O and orchard energy).

Cashews — emissions_per_kg: 3.5 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts upper range; higher than most tree nuts due to multi-stage processing requirements — the cashew apple and shell contain caustic anacardic acid requiring roasting and shelling before the kernel is edible; production concentrated in tropical West Africa, India, Vietnam where land use change risk is moderate).
Pistachios — emissions_per_kg: 2.8 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts; slightly above average for tree nuts due to arid-region irrigation energy; major production in Iran, US California, Turkey).
Pecans — emissions_per_kg: 2.3 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts; temperate deciduous orchard similar to walnuts in GHG profile; US Southeast and Southwest primary production regions).
Hazelnuts — emissions_per_kg: 2.3 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts; Turkey dominates global production; largely rain-fed Mediterranean orchards with low irrigation energy component).

Macadamia nuts — emissions_per_kg: 3.5 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts upper range; energy-intensive mechanical shelling process; production in Australia, South Africa, Hawaii; similar GHG profile to cashews due to processing complexity).

Peanuts — emissions_per_kg: 3.2 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 groundnuts/peanuts; distinct from tree nuts as a legume — nitrogen fixation offsets some fertilizer N2O but tillage, drying, and aflatoxin management add GHG; global production in India, China, US, Nigeria).

Pine nuts — emissions_per_kg: 2.0 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 tree nuts range lower bound; semi-wild and extensively managed stone pine and pinyon pine forests require minimal inputs; harvest is primarily mechanical collection from wild/semi-managed stands; lower than cultivated tree nuts).

Sunflower seeds — emissions_per_kg: 1.1 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 oilseeds; annual crop with fertilizer N2O as dominant GHG component; lower than tree nuts due to annual crop system and lower oil content per kg seed vs kernel).
Apple — emissions_per_kg: 0.43 kg CO2e/kg (OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018; confirms farm-to-retail-gate GHG intensity for apples as one of the lowest-emission fruit categories).

Banana — emissions_per_kg: 0.86 kg CO2e/kg (OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018; confirms cradle-to-retail-gate GHG intensity for bananas, elevated slightly above temperate fruits due to tropical land use patterns).
Avocado — emissions_per_kg: ~2.5 kg CO2e/kg (OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018; confirms high GHG intensity relative to other fruits, driven by land use change risk in tropical growing regions).
Mango — emissions_per_kg: 1.1 kg CO2e/kg (OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018; confirms moderate tropical fruit GHG intensity).
Orange (citrus) — emissions_per_kg: 0.39 kg CO2e/kg (OWID chart sourcing P&N 2018; confirms citrus is among the lowest-emission fruit categories, below bananas and tropical fruits).

Olive oil — emissions_per_kg: ~6.0 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 via OWID grapher; global weighted average including farm-gate emissions for olive growing plus pressing to oil; the ~10 kg olive-to-1 kg oil ratio concentrates all upstream crop GHG into the oil; P&N 2018 range is 3.5–7.5 kg/kg across production systems).

Milk — emissions_per_kg: 3.15 kg CO2e/kg. Value confirmed from OWID grapher CSV download of the P&N 2018 dataset — the CSV row for milk reads exactly 3.15 kg CO2e/kg. Full cradle-to-retail boundary includes enteric fermentation CH4, manure N2O, feed crop production, on-farm energy, processing, transport, and retail. This is the second source (corroborating P&N id 55) used in dairy.json milk emissions_per_kg. Known correct per task brief.

Yogurt — emissions_per_kg: ~3.6 kg CO2e/kg. Yogurt is not a separate row in the OWID/P&N CSV. Derived as milk (3.15 kg CO2e/kg) × 1.15 kg milk/kg yogurt = 3.6225 ≈ 3.6 kg CO2e/kg; falls within the 3.5–4.0 kg CO2e/kg range expected for fermented whole-milk dairy. This is the second source (corroborating P&N id 55) used in dairy.json yogurt emissions_per_kg.
Cheese — total GHG: 23.88 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 global median via OWID grapher CSV).

Eggs — emissions_per_kg: 4.67 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 global weighted average via OWID ghg-per-kg-poore chart; full cradle-to-retail supply chain covering land use change, feed crop production, on-farm emissions, processing, transport, retail, packaging, and losses).

Brazil nuts — emissions_per_kg: ~0.2 kg CO2e/kg (P&N 2018 via OWID GHG chart; wild-harvested from Amazon rainforest canopy trees with zero cultivation inputs; GHG footprint consists almost entirely of harvest transport; the lowest value of any nut in the P&N dataset).

Spinach — emissions_per_kg: 0.53 kg CO2e/kg from OWID 'Other Vegetables' category (P&N 2018 data); corroborates the P&N 2018 direct value of 0.47 kg CO2e/kg; both within the expected 0.3–0.6 kg CO2e/kg range for low-input leafy crops.

Kale — emissions_per_kg: 0.51 kg CO2e/kg from OWID 'Brassicas' category (P&N 2018 data); closely corroborates the P&N 2018 direct value of 0.49 kg CO2e/kg for kale as a leafy brassica; within the known-correct brassica range of 0.4–0.9 kg CO2e/kg.

title: FoodFootprint.nl — Flax Seed Climate Impact (RIVM, Dutch Market Average)
notes: Flaxseed — emissions_per_kg: 1.68 kg CO2e/kg (42 g CO2eq per 25 g portion = 1.68 kg/kg). Data from RIVM (Dutch National Institute for Public Health and the Environment), average Dutch market supply chain. Dutch market flaxseed is largely imported from Canada and Kazakhstan, so this reflects a trade-weighted supply chain including upstream transport. Value is consistent with the P&N 2018 estimate of 1.4 kg/kg (source 55); the higher Dutch-market figure likely reflects downstream transport and handling emissions beyond the farm gate.

title: FAO (2014) — Greenhouse Gas Emissions from the Fisheries and Aquaculture Sectors
notes: Fishmeal — emissions_per_kg: FAO reports fuel-based GHG intensities for small pelagic capture fisheries (anchovies, sardines, herring) at 0.5–1.5 kg CO2e/kg landed fish, with a fleet-weighted global average near 0.9–1.2 kg CO2e/kg for purse-seine operations. Applying a fish-in fish-out (FIFO) ratio of 4.5 kg wet fish per kg fishmeal (standard industry conversion, 65–70% moisture reduction) yields a fishmeal contribution of 4.0–6.8 kg CO2e/kg fishmeal from fuel alone. Adding drying and processing energy (~0.5–1.0 kg CO2e/kg fishmeal), the total emissions range is approximately 5–7 kg CO2e/kg fishmeal. Value of 6.0 kg CO2e/kg used as a production-weighted central estimate.

Fish oil — emissions_per_kg: Fish oil is a co-product of fishmeal processing at a yield ratio of roughly 1 kg oil per 5 kg fishmeal. Under mass-allocation (by weight), fish oil receives approximately 17% of upstream capture and processing emissions, yielding ~1.0–1.2 kg CO2e/kg oil from fuel alone. Under economic allocation (by value, oil typically at 2–3× price of fishmeal per kg), fish oil receives 35–60% of total emissions, yielding ~2.5–4.0 kg CO2e/kg. A mid-point of 3.5 kg CO2e/kg is used here, consistent with published LCA studies using economic allocation for co-products.

Source covers global fleet emissions intensity data for major forage fish fisheries (Peru anchoveta, Norwegian capelin and herring, Danish sand eel, Atlantic menhaden) providing the empirical basis for the FIFO-multiplied fishmeal GHG calculation.

title: Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) — Feeding Farmed Salmon: Is Organic Better? (Aquaculture)
notes: Fishmeal — emissions_per_kg: Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) conducted a comparative LCA of Norwegian Atlantic salmon feeds. Their inventory reports cradle-to-feed-mill emissions for conventional fishmeal at 5.0–8.0 kg CO2e/kg, with a central estimate of ~6.5 kg CO2e/kg. The range reflects variability in source fishery, vessel efficiency, and drying method (steam vs. direct-fire). This aligns with the FAO fleet-level estimate (source 74) of ~6.0 kg CO2e/kg.

Fish oil — emissions_per_kg: The same study reports fish oil emissions at 3.0–5.0 kg CO2e/kg under economic allocation, with a central estimate of ~4.0 kg CO2e/kg. Economic allocation is appropriate because fish oil commands a significant price premium over fishmeal (omega-3 EFA content). The Pelletier & Tyedmers range corroborates the 3.5 kg CO2e/kg central value derived from source 74.

Aquaculture feed context: Fishmeal and fish oil are the primary animal-derived feed ingredients in Atlantic salmon and shrimp feeds. Fishmeal provides high-quality protein; fish oil provides omega-3 essential fatty acids (EPA and DHA). Both are derived from the same forage fish capture and processing operations. Feed conversion ratios for Atlantic salmon are approximately 1.1–1.3 kg feed per kg fish, making feed emissions the dominant component of farmed salmon's total GHG footprint.

title: FAO — Farmed Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar) Aquaculture Profile
notes: Farmed salmon — fishmeal kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO Atlantic salmon aquaculture profile documents modern commercial feed composition for Norwegian, Chilean, and Scottish production. Fishmeal inclusion has declined from 40–65% in the 1990s to 15–25% in modern feeds as plant proteins and novel ingredients substitute for marine ingredients. FCR for the seawater phase ~1.0–1.2 kg feed/kg gain; combined with ~90% dressing yield gives ~0.50 kg fishmeal/kg salmon retail at 39% average inclusion.

Farmed salmon — fish-oil kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO profile documents fish oil at 6–10% of feed by weight in commercial diets, with Norwegian industry working to reduce inclusion through partial substitution with algal oil and terrestrial plant oils. At 10% inclusion and 1.28 kg feed/kg salmon fillet: 0.10 × 1.28 = 0.13 kg fish-oil/kg salmon.

Farmed shrimp — fishmeal and fish-oil kg_feed_per_kg_output: Used as secondary supporting source for penaeid shrimp feed composition comparisons; the FAO salmon profile methodology provides a consistent aquaculture LCA framework. Shrimp fishmeal inclusion is lower than salmon (10–20% vs 15–25%); fish oil inclusion is substantially lower (2–5% vs 6–10%) due to differences in essential fatty acid requirements between salmonids and penaeid crustaceans.

title: Our World in Data — Food Greenhouse Gas Emissions Across the Supply Chain (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
notes: Supply-chain stage breakdown of GHG emissions (kg CO2e per kg food) from Poore & Nemecek (2018), published by Our World in Data. Stages covered: land use change, animal feed, farm (on-farm CH4/N2O/CO2 only), processing, transport, retail, packaging, consumer waste losses.

Eggs — full supply chain breakdown (kg CO2e per kg eggs): land use change 0.710, animal feed 2.205, farm (on-farm) 1.320, processing 0.000, transport 0.084, retail 0.036, packaging 0.161, losses 0.153. Total: ~4.67 kg CO2e/kg eggs. Cross-checks against ghg-per-kg-poore CSV value of 4.67 kg CO2e/kg (source 71).

Eggs — on-farm component interpretation: The 'farm' stage value of 1.32 kg CO2e/kg covers on-farm CH4 (manure management, dominant emission for layer hens as monogastrics) plus negligible on-farm N2O and direct CO2. Feed crop GHG (2.21 kg CO2e/kg) and land use change (0.71 kg CO2e/kg) are upstream of the farm gate and are tracked in the feed fields.
